nameMeanings.name: Rowland
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from the Middle English and Old French personal name Rollant, Rolant, Rolent, Roulent (ancient Germanic Hrodland, Rodland, from hrōd ‘glory’ + land ‘land’), introduced into England by the Normans. It was made famous by French romances about Roland, the most famous of the peers of Charlemagne who was killed at Roncesvalles in AD 778. Although widely used across post-Conquest England (along with Oliver, the name of Roland's companion), it seems to have been restricted to particular gentry families and was never popular. Compare Roland .

English: habitational name from Rowland (Derbyshire) or Rowland Wood in Slinfold (Sussex). The Derbyshire placename derives from Old Norse rá ‘roe, roe buck’ or rá ‘land mark, boundary’ + lúndr ‘small wood, grove’. The Sussex placename probably derives from Middle English roughe ‘rough’ + lond ‘land’ (Old English rūh, land).
English: in northern England and perhaps elsewhere, perhaps a post-medieval variant of   Rawling  . Compare   Rawlinson  ,   Rollinson  .

variants.name: Roland
meanings: French, Walloon, German, English, and Scottish: from an ancient Germanic personal name composed of hrōd ‘fame, renown’ + land ‘land’. In North America, this surname is also an altered form of the more common French variant Rolland . See also English Rowland .

Norwegian (also Røland): habitational name from any of several farms named Rødland mainly in Hordaland or Røyland mainly in Agder, most of them from Old Norse reyrr ‘heap of stones’ or reyrr ‘rush’ (a plant) + the second element land ‘(piece of) land, farmstead’. Compare Rolland .

Norwegian: habitational name from the farm name Roland in Lillehammer, with the first element Old Norse ruth ‘clearing’, or Roland in Agder around Lindesnes, with the first element possibly the Old Norse personal name Rói (either a short form of names beginning with Ro(d)- ‘fame, honor’ or composed with a word for ‘giant’ or ‘pagan priest’). The second element of the farm names is land ‘(piece of) land, farmstead’.

variants.name: Rollison
meanings: English: patronymic from the Middle English personal names Rouland or Roulin; see Rowland , Rolling . Alternatively, and perhaps more likely, the name may be a post-medieval pronunciation of Rawlinson .

variants.name: Orlando
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Angelo, Rocco, Sal, Vito, Antonio, Carlo, Marco, Santo, Carmine, Giovanni, Cosimo.
Italian: from the personal name Orlando, earlier Rolando (see Rowland ).

variants.name: Reuland
meanings: German: from a personal name, the German form of Roland (see Rowland ).

variants.name: Rolling
meanings: English: variant of Rowland , with -ling substituted for -land.

English: habitational name from Rowling Court in Goodnestone (Kent) or Rowling Street in Bilsington (Kent). The origins of these placenames are uncertain, but they may both derive from an unattested Old English word related to an ancient Germanic base rull- ‘something round’ + the Old English placename forming suffix -ing.
English: from the rare Middle English personal name Ro(u)lin, a diminutive of either Roll (Rolf) or Rol(l)and. See Rolfe and Rowland .

variants.name: Rowlands
meanings: English and Welsh: variant of Rowland , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Rowlett
meanings: English: from a diminutive of the personal name Rol(l)and. See Rowland and compare Rolling .
English: from a pet form of the personal name   Ralph  . Compare   Rawling  .
English: from a pet form of the personal name Rolf. See Rolfe and compare Rolling .

variants.name: Rowlan
meanings: English (South London): variant of Rowland with loss of -d.

variants.name: Rouland
meanings: English: variant of   Rowland  .
Americanized form of Norwegian   Rauland  .
In some cases possibly also French: variant of   Roland  .

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

en.lifeSummary: Thomas Rowland was born in 1397, in London, England, United Kingdom. He married Margory Wallace in 1417, in Craigie, East Ayrshire, Scotland, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1472, in his hometown, at the age of 75.
